ch1-ros2/week-1.title: Week 1: Introduction to ROS 2
ch1-ros2/week-1.description: Welcome to the first week of our journey into the Robotic Nervous System! This week, we'll be diving into the fundamentals of ROS 2, the open-source middleware that has become the standard for robotics development.

ch1-ros2/week-2.title: Week 2: ROS 2 Nodes and Topics
ch1-ros2/week-2.description: This week, we'll explore two of the most fundamental concepts in ROS 2: Nodes and Topics. They are the building blocks of any ROS 2 application.

ch1-ros2/week-3.title: Week 3: ROS 2 Services and Actions
ch1-ros2/week-3.description: While topics are great for continuous data streams, sometimes we need a request/reply interaction. This is where Services and Actions come in.

ch1-ros2/week-4.title: Week 4: Launch Files and Parameters
ch1-ros2/week-4.description: As our robotics systems grow in complexity, running each node manually becomes tedious. Launch files allow us to start and configure a collection of nodes at once. Parameters allow us to configure nodes without recompiling our code.

ch2-digital-twin/week-1.title: Week 1: Introduction to Simulation
ch2-digital-twin/week-1.description: In robotics, simulation is an indispensable tool. It allows us to design, test, and iterate on our robot's hardware and software in a virtual environment before deploying it in the real world. This saves time, money, and reduces the risk of damaging expensive hardware.

ch2-digital-twin/week-2.title: Week 2: Gazebo Simulation
ch2-digital-twin/week-2.description: Gazebo is a mature, open-source robotics simulator. It is the go-to choice for many roboticists, especially those working with ROS. Gazebo provides a rich set of features for simulating robots, sensors, and environments.

ch2-digital-twin/week-3.title: Week 3: Unity Simulation
ch2-digital-twin/week-3.description: Unity is a powerful and versatile game engine that has gained traction in the robotics community for its high-fidelity graphics, ease of use, and extensive asset store. It is particularly well-suited for training and testing computer vision and reinforcement learning models.

ch2-digital-twin/week-4.title: Week 4: ROS 2 and Unity Integration
ch2-digital-twin/week-4.description: The real power of using Unity for robotics simulation comes from its ability to connect with ROS 2. This allows us to leverage the vast ecosystem of ROS 2 tools and packages while taking advantage of Unity's advanced rendering capabilities.

ch3-nvidia-isaac/week-1.title: Week 1: Introduction to NVIDIA Isaac
ch3-nvidia-isaac/week-1.description: Welcome to Chapter 3! Now that we have a grasp of ROS 2 and simulation, it's time to build the "brain" of our robot. For this, we turn to the NVIDIA Isaac platform, a powerful toolkit for developing AI-powered robots.

ch3-nvidia-isaac/week-2.title: Week 2: Isaac Sim
ch3-nvidia-isaac/week-2.description: NVIDIA Isaac Sim is a scalable robotics simulation application and synthetic data generation tool. It is built on NVIDIA Omniverse™, a platform for building and operating 3D virtual worlds. Isaac Sim provides a realistic and physically accurate environment for developing, testing, and training AI-based robots.

ch3-nvidia-isaac/week-3.title: Week 3: Isaac Gym
ch3-nvidia-isaac/week-3.description: NVIDIA Isaac Gym is a high-performance reinforcement learning (RL) platform. It is designed to train RL agents for robotic tasks at a massive scale by leveraging the parallel processing power of NVIDIA GPUs.

ch3-nvidia-isaac/week-4.title: Week 4: Isaac ROS
ch3-nvidia-isaac/week-4.description: NVIDIA Isaac ROS is a collection of hardware-accelerated packages that enable ROS developers to build high-performance robotics applications. It bridges the gap between the powerful NVIDIA Isaac SDKs and the familiar ROS 2 ecosystem.

ch4-vla/week-1.title: Week 1: Introduction to VLAs
ch4-vla/week-1.description: Welcome to the final chapter of our textbook! We've built a nervous system (ROS 2), created a virtual world (Gazebo/Unity), and given our robot a powerful brain (NVIDIA Isaac). Now, it's time to connect the robot's perception to its actions using Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models.

ch4-vla/week-2.title: Week 2: VLA Architectures
ch4-vla/week-2.description: Vision-Language-Action models come in various architectures, but they generally share a common structure: an encoder-decoder framework, often based on the Transformer architecture that has revolutionized natural language processing.

ch4-vla/week-3.title: Week 3: Training VLAs
ch4-vla/week-3.description: Training a Vision-Language-Action model is a complex process that requires a large amount of demonstration data. The most common training paradigm is Behavior Cloning (BC).

ch4-vla/week-4.title: Week 4: Deploying VLAs on Robots
ch4-vla/week-4.description: Once a Vision-Language-Action model is trained, the next step is to deploy it on a physical robot. This is often referred to as the "inference" phase.

intro.title: Tutorial Intro
intro.description: Let's discover Docusaurus in less than 5 minutes.
